runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 9 in a U.S. microsimulation model context.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. Decile 9 represents high-income individuals who may have some flexibility in hours or effort but face significant institutional constraints. Empirical literature and policy simulation defaults typically cluster between 0.1 and 0.2 for this group.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 9 in a U.S. microsimulation model context.",
  "point_estimate": 0.15,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.05,
    "p25": 0.1,
    "p50": 0.15,
    "p75": 0.25,
    "p95": 0.45
  },
  "citations": [
    "Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012)",
    "CBO (2012) Labor Supply Substitution Elasticities",
    "Blundell and MaCurdy (1999)"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. Decile 9 represents high-income individuals who may have some flexibility in hours or effort but face significant institutional constraints. Empirical literature and policy simulation defaults typically cluster between 0.1 and 0.2 for this group."
}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution elasticity for individuals in the 9th earnings decile within a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation context.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. In the 9th decile, earners often have high attachment to the labor force and significant human capital, leading to modest substitution effects on the intensive margin. However, some responsiveness remains due to flexibility in hours or effort, especially toward the upper end of the decile where tax salience increases.
